coredata.dc:title: Efficient color-tunable room temperature phosphorescence through confining carbon dots in ionic crystal
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Changchang
Zhao, Xinyang
Li, Chao
Hu, Junhua
Zhu, Jinyang
Lou, Qing
Chen, Niu
Song, Zhijiang
Chen, Xu
Pan, Gencai
coredata.dc:description: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials are highly attractive in many fields, but it is still a great challenge to obtain RTP materials with color tunability. Herein, we report a new type of multicolor phosphorescence carbon dots (CDs) through confining CDs into rigid crystal network through the introduction of ionic bonds. The RTP color can rationally vary from green (553 nm) to orange-red (640 nm) under different excitation wavelengths. More importantly, the obtained CDs can also maintain their RTP emission in ethanol solution. The excitation-dependent property was owing to the existence of polychromatic emission centers on the surface of CDs, and the generation of RTP can be attributed to the ionic bond in CDs system to suppress non-radiative transition of triplet states. Based on this unique multicolor RTP phenomenon, the CDs were successfully applied in anticounterfeiting and light-emitting diodes. This finding not only provides a new strategy for the preparation of multicolor RTP materials but also reveals a great potential of CDs in developing novel optical materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Room temperature phosphorescence
Multiple emission centers
Ionic crystal
Colloidal solution

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Advanced information encryption and LEDs application

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the formation of u-CDs@NaOH (top). The photographs of u-CDs@NaOH in powder (bottom: a-c) and in ethanol solution (bottom: d-f) after the irradiation (365, 405 and 450 nm) just turning off.

(a) Photographs of u-CDs@NaOH powder at different delay times with the corresponding irradiations on and off. (b) RTP emission spectra of u-CDs@NaOH powder with various excitation wavelengths (i.e., 365, 405, 450 nm and white-light) were turned off. (c) CIE chromaticity diagram, (d) RTP decay spectra, (e) RTP (bottom) and PL (top) quantum yields (QYs) of u-CDs@NaOH powder with various excitation wavelengths (i.e., 365, 405 and 450 nm).

(a) TEM image, (b) size distribution, (c) HR-TEM images, (d) XRD, (e) FT-IR, (f) XPS spectra of u-CDs@NaOH. (g-i) High-resolution XPS spectra and fitting results of C 1 s (g), O 1 s (h), and Na 1 s (i) of u-CDs@NaOH.
The proposed mechanism for the enhanced multicolor RTP emission of u-CDs@NaOH.

(a) RTP emission spectra of u-CDs@NaOH powders freshly prepared (red line) and hermetically stored for 7 days (blue line) with different light irradiations (i.e., 365, 405, 450 nm and white-light) were turned off (Insert: photographs with the corresponding irradiations were turned on). (b) RTP emission spectra of u-CDs@NaOH in ethanol solution with various wavelengths (i.e., 365, 405 and 450 nm) were turned off. (c) The RTP emission stability of u-CDs@NaOH in ethanol solution.

(a) Images of “heart” patterns which were fabricated from u-CDs@NaOH powder after the different excitation wavelengths (365, 405, 450 nm and white-light) were turned on and off. (b) Photographs of RTP emission from the integrated LED devices after power-up and outage of electricity.

head.ce:title: Efficient color-tunable room temperature phosphorescence through confining carbon dots in ionic crystal

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: International Joint Research Laboratory of New Energy Materials and Devices of Henan Province, School of Physics and Electronics, Henan University, Kaifeng, 475004, P. R. China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials are highly attractive in many fields, but it is still a great challenge to obtain RTP materials with color tunability. Herein, we report a new type of multicolor phosphorescence carbon dots (CDs) through confining CDs into rigid crystal network through the introduction of ionic bonds. The RTP color can rationally vary from green (553 nm) to orange-red (640 nm) under different excitation wavelengths. More importantly, the obtained CDs can also maintain their RTP emission in ethanol solution. The excitation-dependent property was owing to the existence of polychromatic emission centers on the surface of CDs, and the generation of RTP can be attributed to the ionic bond in CDs system to suppress non-radiative transition of triplet states. Based on this unique multicolor RTP phenomenon, the CDs were successfully applied in anticounterfeiting and light-emitting diodes. This finding not only provides a new strategy for the preparation of multicolor RTP materials but also reveals a great potential of CDs in developing novel optical materials.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: By embedding CDs into the ionic crystal, the rotation and vibration of the chromophore can be effectively prevented, and the strong ionic bond formed in CDs system can effectively suppress the triplet nonradiative transition, finally causes the enhancement of multi-color RTP phenomenon.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Multicolor luminescent materials have received much attention in recent years owing to their potential applications, such as in information encryption, multicolor display and multimodal anticounterfeiting [1–6]. Among them, room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have shown more advantages in the fields of time-resolved bioimaging, optoelectronic devices and document security, et al. [7,8] To realize effective RTP emission, two crucial factors should primarily be solved. One is to obtain an efficient intersystem crossing (ISC) rate from singlet state (S1) to excited triplet (T1) to populate enough triplet excitons and the other is to suppress or reduce the nonradiative transition process of triplet exciton. Based on these two principles, strategies including H-aggregation [9,10], host-guest doping [11,12], crystal engineering [13], metal-organic frameworks [14], halogen bonding [15] and other methods are proposed to achieve RTP through improving the ISC process or suppressing the non-radiation transitions of triplet excitons [16]. Although significant breakthroughs have been achieved to obtain RTP materials, it is still a great challenge to obtain RTP materials with color tunability in one sample.

Carbon dots (CDs) as a new class of luminescent materials have attracted increasing interest because of their excellent optical performance, photostability, low toxicity and easy synthesis [17–20]. Especially, excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) properties of the CDs, make it possible to generate multicolor RTP. Up to now, extensive attempts have been made to achieve multicolor RTP materials, for example, CDs powder with color-tunable (500–575 nm) were successfully prepared by Jiang et al. [21], and they pointed that tangled carbon chains may behave as matrices to immobilize the emitting centers and stabilize the excited triplet species by forming hydrogen bonds, which play a key role in achieving RTP. Shi et al. reported a type of color-tunable RTP CDs through limiting domain surface color group, and its longest wavelength can reach about 630 nm [22]. Despite these inspiring achievements, there is seldom report about color-tunable RTP CDs in colloidal solution.

Herein, we report a method to embed CDs in ionic crystal to realize a color-tunable RTP through modifying seed CDs with urea, HCl, and sodium hydroxide (NaOH). Phosphorescence color of CDs can be reasonably turned from green (553 nm) to orange-red (640 nm) by changing excitation wavelength from 360 to 450 nm. More importantly, the obtained CDs can maintain their RTP emission in ethanol solution. Optical and structural characterization of CDs show that the multicolor RTP phenomenon mainly origins from the following two reasons: on the one hand, the addition of HCl and urea enriches the surface chemical groups (such as CO, CN, -NH2) of CDs, and thus generate multicolor luminescent centers [23]; On the other hand, the strong ionic bonds in CDs system can effectively prevent the rotation and vibration of the chromophore and suppress the triplet non-radiative transition [24]. Finally, the RTP CDs can be successfully applied to anti-counterfeiting and light-emitting diodes (LEDs).

All reagents were used as received without further purification. Citric acid (99.5%), HCl (38%), ethanol and NaOH was purchased from Beijing Chemical Works. Urea (99.5%) was purchased from Aladdin Company Limited (Shang Hai).

2 g urea and 1 g citric acid were dissolved in deionized water (10 mL), then the solution was transferred into a poly(tetraﬂuoroethylene) (Teﬂon)-lined autoclave and sealed carefully. The autoclave (50 mL) reacted at 160 ℃ for 8 h. After that, the obtained dark brown solution purified in a 3500-dialysis bag. And then dried in an oven at 60 °C to give the dark products of seed CDs.

1.0 g urea, 1.0 mL HCl and 3.0 mg seed CDs were dissolved in 10 mL ethanol solution. Then the solution was transferred into a poly(tetraﬂuoroethylene) (Teﬂon)-lined autoclave (50 mL) and reacted at 160 °C for 6 h. The obtained solution was cooled down to room temperature and purified in a 3500-dialysis bag before drying in an oven at 60 °C to provide the resulting powdered u-CDs sample.

100 mg u-CDs powder, 100 mg urea and 100 mg NaOH were dissolved in 10 mL water solution in a beaker by shaking for 10 mins to completely dissolve the urea and CDs. After that, the beaker was put into an oil bath at 150 °C for 2 h to obtain u-CDs@NaOH powder.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution microscopy observations were performed with a JEOL-2100 F microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with an ESCALab220i-XL electron spectrometer from VG Scientific. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured with a Shimadzu UV-3101PC UV–vis scanning spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence and RTP emission spectra of CDs were measured on a QE Pro (Ocean Insight); Phosphorescence lifetimes decay profiles were measured on an Edinburgh FLS 980 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The absolute photoluminescence quantum yields (PL QYs) were measured an Edinburgh FLS 980 fluorescence spectrophotometer with an integrating sphere under ambient conditions.

The preparation process of multicolor RTP CDs is shown in Fig. 1 (top). Briefly, 3 mg seed CDs (seed CDs that were prepared in our previous work) [25], 1 g urea, 1 mL concentrated HCl and 10 mL ethanol were heated at 160 °C for 6 h in an autoclave. The obtained solution was dialyzed with deionized water through a dialysis membrane (3500 Da) for 12 h and dried in an oven at 60 °C to prepare u-CDs (the prefix u denotes the urea). Then, u-CDs were further modified with urea and NaOH to obtain u-CDs@NaOH. (Details see the experimental section). As shown in Fig. 1 (bottom: a-c), u-CDs@NaOH show multicolor RTP in powder, ranging from green to orange-red with changing the excitation wavelength from 365 to 450 nm. More importantly, u-CDs@NaOH also maintain bright RTP emission in ethanol solution (Fig. 1 bottom: d-f).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. S1a) image exhibits that u-CDs were uniform nanoparticles with an average diameter of 5.2 nm (inset in the Fig. S1a). High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image shows u-CDs have well-resolved lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. S1b), which is ascribed to the (100) plane of graphite [26]. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum (Fig. S1c) indicates that u-CDs possess plenty of surface chemical groups such as -NH2, -OH, CO/CN and C-N et al. [27] The full survey spectrum of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. S1d) reveals the presence of C 1 s (284.4 eV), N 1 s (399.2 eV), O 1 s (530.6 eV) and Cl 2p (199.5 eV) elementals with contents to be 52.37%, 18.99%, 18.86% and 9.77%, respectively [28,29]. Then, the UV–vis absorption spectrum and PL emission spectra of u-CDs powder were monitored. Fig. S2a shows two absorption peaks at 295 and 341 nm, which are assigned to the π→π * of carbon core and n→π * transition of CO, respectively [30–32]. As shown in Fig. S2b, the u-CDs powder show a strong peak at 463 nm (blue emission) when excited under 365 nm wavelength, and the emission wavelengths also redshift with increased excitation wavelengths. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S3, a very weak RTP emission can be observed from u-CDs powder, which may be attributed to the formed intramolecular hydrogen bonding to inhibit non-radiative transition of triplet state [33,34]. According to the previous reports, the addition of HCl in the reaction solution can alter the emission color because the involvement of acids improves the degree of carbonization and facilitates the doping of heteroatoms [35]. Besides that, urea molecules can react with the carbon precursors and other reagents to form a variety of nitrogen/oxygen-related functional groups on the surface of the CDs (such as CO, CN, or -NH2) [36]. On the one hand, the introduction of nitrogen/oxygen-related groups probably endow CDs with different emission centers and further induce multiple emission peaks [37]. On the other hand, the CO/CN groups are known to be able to improve spin-orbit coupling and facilitate the ISC process, so effectively populating triplet excitons [38]. In addition, these functional groups could play roles in forming intraparticle hydrogen bonds for stabilization of the excited triplet species, thus endow CDs with phosphorescent properties [39]. However, the strength of hydrogen bonds was relatively weak, thereby their effect on stabilizing triplet states was limited [40].

Then, the photophysical features of u-CDs@NaOH powder were systematically investigated. The u-CDs@NaOH powder exhibited an excitation-dependent feature, the corresponding PL emission centers were located at 490, 510 and 527 nm under the excitation wavelengths of 365, 405 and 450 nm, respectively (Fig. S4). More importantly, enhanced multicolor RTP was observed from the u-CDs@NaOH powder. As shown in Fig. 2a, b and Video S1, the RTP emission can vary from green to orange-red (553–640 nm) with different excitation wavelengths (i.e., 365, 405 and 450 nm). We also found that under white-light excitation, the u-CDs@NaOH powder showed bright RTP emission centered at 630 nm. In order to explanation how the white-light excites u-CDs@NaOH and leads RTP emission at 630 nm, we measured the PL emission spectrum of white-light excitation source. As shown in Fig. S5, white-light excitation source emitted broad PL emission from blue to red region with two main emission peaks at around 440 and 570 nm. More importantly, under 450 nm light excitation, u-CDs@NaOH powder also shows a bright red RTP emission but centered at around 640 nm, which is like that of white-light excitation. Therefore, we speculate that the 440 nm light in the white-light composition leads the RTP emission of u-CDs@NaOH at 630 nm. In addition, from the Commission International de l′Eclairage (CIE) coordinate diagram (Fig. 2c), the coordinate of multicolor RTP emission gradually changes from (0.39, 0.48) to (0.48, 0.46) and to (0.54, 0.40) with CCT of 4198, 2735 and 1782 K, respectively, which is consistent with the phenomenon observed by the naked eye.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2023.169674.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S1.
Video S1
.

Additionally, the time-resolved decay curves of u-CDs@NaOH powder was given in Fig. 2d and Table S1, which can be fitted by a biexponential function. According to the following equation (Eq. 1) [41,42]:
(1)
τ
avg
= Σ
α
i
τ
i
2
/ Σ
α
i
τ
i
its average phosphorescence lifetimes were calculated to be 423.43 ms (Ex.= 365 nm, Em.= 553 nm), 257.12 ms (Ex.= 405 nm, Em.= 606 nm) and 220.82 ms (Ex.= 450 nm, Em.= 640 nm), respectively. The multiple lifetimes imply that several emitting species are represented, which may be caused by a variety of chemical environments on the surface of u-CDs@NaOH [43]. Furthermore, compared with u-CDs, the corresponding PL QYs of u-CDs@NaOH reached up to 11.47%, 13.52% and 14.15% under excitation wavelengths of 365, 405 and 450 nm, respectively (Fig. 2e: top and Fig. S6). By reference to the reported method (Fig. S7) [44], phosphorescence QYs of u-CDs@NaOH powder were calculated to be 3.68%, 4.33%, 2.16% (Fig. 2e bottom). Then, to evaluate the energy gap (ΔEST) between the lowest triplet (T1) and the singlet (S1), we measured the PL and phosphorescence spectra of the u-CDs@NaOH powder at 77 K with the 365 nm UV-light was turned on and off (Fig. S8). Accordingly, the ΔEST value was calculated to be 0.20 eV (S1 =2.54 eV and T1 =2.34 eV, respectively), and such a small value benefits an effective ISC process to populate triplet excitons [45].

To further explore the reasons for the RTP enhancement of u-CDs@NaOH, the morphology and chemical compositions of u-CDs@NaOH were characterized. As shown in Fig. 3a, u-CDs@NaOH exhibit well-dispersed spherical shapes with an average size of about 4.2 nm (Fig. 3b). The decrease in particle size compared with u-CDs may be due to the surface defects of CDs consumed after NaOH modification [46]. Moreover, HR-TEM images show that u-CDs@NaOH have well-resolved lattice fringes with two types of 0.28 nm and 0.24 nm (Fig. 3c), which are ascribed to the (200) plane of NaCl and the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [46,47]. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern shows that the u-CDs@NaOH has obvious diffraction peaks (Fig. 3d), in which the characteristic diffraction peaks at 27.3°, 31.7°, 45.4°, 56.5° and 75.3° can be indexed to the (111), (200), (220), (222) and (420) crystal planes of NaCl (JCPDS No. 05–0628), respectively [48]. Besides, the XRD spectrum also shows a broad distinct characteristic peak at 21.0°, which is attributed to the graphitic structure with an interlayer spacing (002) of 0.41 nm [49]. The above discussion further confirmed that the CDs were successfully embedded into the rigid crystal network.

The surface functional groups of u-CDs@NaOH were detected by FT-IR spectrometer. As shown in Fig. 3e, the broad absorption band from 2500 to 3800 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of -OH, -NH2 and -CH2 [21] . The absorption peaks at 3158, 3042, 2781, 1718, 1580, 1406 and 1065 cm−1 could be assigned to the N-H, -CH2-, C-H, CO, COOH, C-N and C-O, respectively [21,26,50,51]. Moreover, the obvious absorption peaks at 705 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of the C-Cl bond [52]. Those existence of abundant groups on the surface of u-CDs@NaOH was responsible for their excitation-dependent behavior. The FT-IR results can be further confirmed by the XPS analyses. As shown in Fig. 3f and Table S2, the u-CDs@NaOH mainly contain C, N, O, Na and Cl elements with atomic percentages of 46.98%, 3.10%, 33.98%, 14.98% and 0.96%, respectively. More in detail, the HR-XPS spectrum of C 1 s reveals the presence of CN/CO (288.9 eV), C-O/C-N (285.6 eV) and CC/C-C (284.6 eV) (Fig. 3g) [53]. The O 1 s spectrum is divided into three peaks at 535.5, 531.7 and 530.8 eV, attributed to Na KLL [54], CO and C-O bonds, respectively (Fig. 3h). Fig. 3i shows the high-resolution spectrum of Na 1 s, which exposes the abundant formation of Na+ in the CDs system [39]. According to the above results, the multiple ionic bonds formed on the surface of u-CDs@NaOH sample may play an important role in achieving enhanced multicolor RTP emission.

Taking the above results together, the achieved enhanced multicolor RTP emission of u-CDs@NaOH may be proposed as follows ( Fig. 4). As shown in Fig. 4a, the RTP emission can be observed from u-CDs powder, which may be attributed to the formed intramolecular hydrogen bonding to inhibit non-radiative transition of triplet state [55]. However, the strength of hydrogen bonds was relatively weak, thereby their effect on stabilizing triplet states was limited [40]. For u-CDs@NaOH, on the one hand, the carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs can be ionized in the presence of NaOH, and ionic bonds render CDs with a firmly rigid environment (Fig. 4b), which can further suppress the non-radiative transitions and thus successfully stabilize triplet excited states of the CDs to largely boost their RTP intensity [21,22]. On the other hand, the introduction of nitrogen/oxygen-related groups endow CDs with different emission centers, and thus induced multiple RTP emission in the u-CDs@NaOH system (Fig. 4c).

The RTP emission stability of u-CDs@NaOH powder were measured. As shown in Fig. 5a, for 7 days in hermetic storage at ambient conditions, the phosphorescence emitting spectra of u-CDs@NaOH powder under different light irradiations (i.e., 365, 405, 450 nm and white-light) has an insignificant change, indicating u-CDs@NaOH powder has excellent stability. According to the previous reports, phosphorescence is rarely observed in water due to the significant dissipation of triplet states which mainly through the following two paths: vibration/rotation and dissolved oxygen [56]. Thus, efficient phosphorescence from colloidal soluble CDs can be achieved if the mentioned issues were addressed. As shown in Fig. S9a the u-CDs@NaOH water solution displayed blue PL under irradiation by 365 nm UV-light, and no RTP emission was observed after removing the excitation source (Fig. S9b), which can be attributed to the dissolved oxygen in water and water destructs of the ionic bond [52]. Astonishingly, as shown in Fig. 5b and video S2, u-CDs@NaOH display bright RTP emission in ethanol solution, which may be attributed to seldom vibration/rotation and dissolved oxygen when u-CDs@NaOH in ethanol. Moreover, we also tested the stability of the u-CDs@NaOH in ethanol solution. As shown in Fig. 5c, the emission intensities from u-CDs@NaOH ethanol solution only change slightly (<8%,<11%,<13%, respectively) after hermetic storage 7 days at ambient conditions, further expands its application prospect.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2023.169674.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S2.
Video S2
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advanced information encryption and LEDs application
ce:section.ce:para: Given the excitation-dependent colorful RTP feature of the u-CDs@NaOH, we further demonstrate its applications in information encryption and LEDs. Fig. 6a depicts distinctive dual-modal switching encryption by loading u-CDs@NaOH powder into a “heart” mold. When exposed to various light excitations, different PL colors were readily readout visible, and once the excitation was switched off, the phosphorescence color could be changed from green to orange-red. Such unique photo-stimulated polychromic RTP phenomena are more difficult to be mimicked or forged, and believed to be a promising strategy in high-level anti-counterfeiting. In recently, CDs with fluorescence/phosphorescence dual emission properties have aroused increasing attention owing to their low cost, low toxicity, high efficiency, demonstrating their potential application in alternating current LEDs and time delay LEDs [57–60]. Benefiting from the efficient multicolor RTP emission, time delay LEDs were fabricated, which can stay lit for other illumination time and give eyes a buffer time to reduce transient blindness because of the abrupt darkness of switching off. As shown in Fig. 6b, with different wavelength LED chips (365, 395 and 430 nm) as excitation source, the integrated LED devices displayed visual RTP emission after the stoppage of electricity, which colors changed from green to orange and orange-red. In view of the low-cost, eco-friendly, and facile synthesis, u-CDs@NaOH will be promising in a variety of advanced optical applications.

In summary, a facile method for the preparation of multicolor RTP CDs was proposed by embedding CDs into rigid crystal network through the introduction of ionic bonds. The as-obtained CDs powder, that is, u-CDs@NaOH, not only exhibits remarkably multicolor RTP emission from 553 nm to 640 nm with changing the excitation wavelength from 365 to 450 nm, but also maintains its RTP emission in ethanol solution. The multicolor RTP for the u-CDs@NaOH could be attributed to the existence of multiple emission centers and ionic bond in CDs system. The addition of urea in the precursor aqueous solution enriches the surface chemical groups of CDs, and generate multicolor luminescent centers. The strong ionic bonds in CDs system can effectively prevent the rotation and vibration of the chromophore and suppress the triplet non-radiative transition. Based on the unique optical features, u-CDs@NaOH could be applied in the fields of advanced anti-counterfeiting and LED devices.

Changchang Li: Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Jinyang Zhu: Funding acquisition, Investigation, Supervision. Gencai Pan: Funding acquisition, Investigation, Supervision. Junhua Hu: Funding acquisition, Investigation, Supervision. Xinyang Zhao: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Chao Li, Qing Lou: Review and revise the article. Niu Chen: Sort out data and analyze. Zhijiang Song: Sort out data and analyze. Xu Chen: Sort out data and analyze.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (12004345, 12004346, 12174087), the Open Fund of the State Key Laboratory of Integrated Optoelectronics (No. IOSKL2020KF08), the Program for Innovative Research Team (in Science and Technology) in University of Henan Province (21IRTSTHN003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2023.169674.

.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Organic persistent luminescent materials: ultralong room-temperature phosphorescence and multicolor-tunable afterglow
ACS Appl. Mater. Inter.
